_meta.issue: #112-#118 [M15] / #124 [M16] Formative path S4 — Holak Scale (training module, expansion)
_meta.interaction: Data and use-case classification (kind: classify) — formative exercise, NOT scored toward completion
_meta.source: Holak Scale maturity axis: security, data privacy and governance of AI use (including integrations/MCP). Attribution: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka.
_meta.dataPolicy: ALL examples SYNTHETIC and generic (no real data/PII). Formative module — no pass threshold, no certificate.

intro: Mature AI adoption is not only about effectiveness but about data hygiene: what you may give the model, what you must anonymize first, and what you must never use — including when AI reaches for data through integrations and MCP. This module trains you to classify data and set minimal governance rules. It is a growth exercise, not a test.

screens.title: Three data bins: allowed, to anonymize, disallowed

blocks.text: Before you paste anything into a model, assign the data to one of three bins. Allowed: public or synthetic data. To anonymize: data that could point to a person or system but is safe once masked. Disallowed: secrets and sensitive data that must never be exposed.

items: To anonymize — remove or replace with a placeholder anything that identifies a person/system.
Disallowed — passwords, keys, secrets, sensitive data with no basis for use.

blocks.title: A Holak Scale axis
blocks.text: A written acceptable-use policy (data, privacy, IP) and a minimal audit trail are a mark of mature governance.

screens.title: Integrations and MCP: the data boundary moves further

blocks.text: When you connect AI to tools through integrations or MCP (Model Context Protocol), the model no longer sees only what you paste — it can reach files, databases, or emails. This is convenient and powerful, but the data boundary moves: you must deliberately limit what the integration can access.

items: Least-access principle — an integration sees only what the task requires.
Filter or mask sensitive data BEFORE it enters the model, not after.
Treat every integration as a new data input — cover it with the same policy.

blocks.title: The „just a test” trap
blocks.text: „I'll connect an integration to production only once, just to check” is the most common leak. Data exposed once may already be out of your control — always limit access and mask, even in an experiment.

Next you will assign data to bins: allowed, to anonymize, disallowed. The order of items is random, so content matters, not a pattern. No pass threshold — the point is the reflex of classifying data before it reaches the model or an integration.

interaction.title: Allowed, to anonymize, or disallowed?
interaction.intro: Assign each data case to the right bin. Feedback explains the privacy and governance rule.

categories.label: To anonymize
categories.desc: Safe only after identifiers are masked.

categories.label: Disallowed
categories.desc: Secrets and sensitive data with no basis for use.

items.text: A public fragment of an open-source project's documentation.
items.rationale: Public, non-identifying data — fine to use.

items.text: Made-up, synthetic test data with no link to a real person.
items.rationale: Synthetic data is safe by definition.

items.text: A bug report with a user login that you replace with a placeholder.
items.rationale: Remove/mask the identifier — then it is safe.

items.text: Logs with customer contact details that must be masked before use.
items.rationale: After anonymization the data no longer points to a person.

items.text: Passwords and access keys to a production system.
items.rationale: Secrets never go into a prompt or integration.

items.text: An MCP integration with full access to the production customer database „for convenience”.
items.rationale: Excessive integration access to sensitive data — violates least access.

items.text: An export of tickets with personal data masked, given for analysis.
items.rationale: Masking before entering the model makes the data safe.

items.text: A customer's full personal data pasted directly into a public model.
items.rationale: Identifying sensitive data with no basis or anonymization — disallowed.

points: Before sending, assign every piece of data to a bin: allowed / to anonymize / disallowed.
Integrations and MCP move the data boundary — apply least access and mask before data enters the model.
Write a simple policy and keep a minimal trail of AI use in critical processes.
checklist: Can I assign my typical data to the three bins?
Do my integrations/MCP have only the minimal access needed?
Do I know what must never be exposed (secrets, sensitive data)?
summary.nextStep: Next step: write a one-page rule set for your team: allowed use cases, anonymization, minimal integration access, no secrets, a simple audit trail.
